title: Modern Kid's Picnic Table and Benches
summary: A child-sized picnic set with mitered box legs and slatted tops that drain water. Two separate benches instead of attached seats — easier for little kids to get in and out.
description:
This is a kid-scaled picnic table with two loose benches, built in a clean modern style rather than the classic A-frame. The legs are hollow mitered boxes made from 1x4 — four boards mitered at 45° and joined into a square-section leg — which looks far more finished than a plain 2x4 leg and hides all the fasteners inside.

The design is quietly smart about outdoor durability. The tops (both table and benches) are slatted with a 1/2" gap between boards and framed by 2x2 ends, so water drains straight through and there are no flat pockets where it can sit and rot the wood. That detail is worth preserving if you substitute materials — a solid top would trap water and fail years sooner.

Separate benches instead of attached seats is a deliberate choice for small children: they can climb in and out without straddling a fixed seat, and you can pull the benches away to wipe up. The mitered legs are the only part that needs care — cut the 45s accurately and fasten from the insides of the legs to hide the screws. Use exterior screws and an outdoor finish (or a rot-resistant species) if it lives outside; the plan uses standard lumber, so upgrade for longevity.

tools.note: The 45° miters on the leg boards are the whole trick — accuracy here matters.
Kids' hands and legs — no splinters.

materials.name: Wood glue (waterproof)
materials.note: Type III / exterior for an outdoor build.

cutList.note: Ends mitered 45°; 20-1/2" short-point to long-point. Review layout before cutting.
Both ends mitered 45°. Review layout before cutting.
Cut to measurement, one end mitered back 45°.

steps.title: Build the table legs
steps.body: Review the leg layout before cutting, then cut the leg boards with 45° miters so the short-point to long-point measures 20-1/2". Mark all four legs as shown, and fasten the leg boards together with glue and 1-1/4" screws into mitered box legs. Drive the screws from the insides of the legs so the holes don't show on the finished faces.

steps.title: Fill in the legs
steps.body: Measure and cut the remaining leg fill pieces to complete the box-leg sections, and fasten with glue and 1-1/4" screws, again hiding the screw holes on the inside. A fully closed box leg is stiffer and reads as solid.

steps.title: Build the tabletop and attach the legs
steps.body: Fasten the 2x2 ends to the tabletop boards with glue and 2-1/2" screws, leaving a 1/2" gap between the boards — that gap is what drains water and keeps the top from rotting. Then attach the ends to the legs with 2-1/2" screws. The design leaves no flat pockets for water to sit, so keep those gaps.

steps.title: Build the bench legs
steps.body: Build the two sets of bench legs exactly as you built the table legs in step 1, mitered and boxed, using glue and 1-1/4" screws with the holes hidden inside.

steps.title: Build the bench seats and finish
steps.body: Build the two bench seats the same way as the tabletop — 2x2 ends fastened to the 2x4 seat boards with 1/2" drainage gaps — then attach the legs. Fill screw holes, sand every edge and corner smooth for little hands and legs, and apply an exterior finish. Loose benches let small kids climb in and out and let you pull everything apart to clean.

images.alt: Child-sized modern picnic table with two separate benches
